prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response: “Une Chanson Douce” by Henri Salvador: a 1950s French lullaby so weightless that its first quiet “do-do l’enfant do” feels like someone laying a hand on my chest. The lyric is only a list of tender instructions—close your eyes, the birds are already asleep—but the way the syllables float over muted jazz guitar makes every word sound like a promise that the world will still be gentle when I wake up.
